prior_learning: Year 7: - Spring 1: writing to inform and advise/ Summer 1: writing to persuade
Year 8 - Autumn 2: writing to persuade and review/ Spring 2: writing to advise and inform
Year 9 - (Currently Spring 2): writing to argue and persuade
common_misconceptions: Reports are just a summary of information: reports should not only summarise the information but also analyse and evaluate it. Students should avoid simply listing information without offering any analysis or evaluation.
Reports should be written in a casual style: reports should be written in a formal style with proper grammar, punctuation, and sentence structure.
Reports do not require evidence: reports should be evidence-based and include facts, statistics, and examples to support their points.
Reports should not have opinions: a report can be strengthened by incorporating the student's perspective and analysis.

Reports do not require planning: planning is an important step in the report writing process. Students should consider the purpose of the report, the audience, and the structure of the report before starting to write.

learning_outcomes: I can identify the most important information in a report
I can understand the importance of balance.
key_knowledge_components: To know how to determine the most important information and events in a report.
To know the importance of balance in a report.

teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Discuss the main features of Victorian-era schooling, including discipline, rote learning, and the use of corporal punishment. Students will look at a range of sources about Victorian-era schooling such as diary entries, letters and newspapers Students work in groups to discuss the directed questions and share their ideas with the rest of the class. Handouts with discussion questions to aid note taking and understanding Assessment: ORACY: Lead a…
